explanation: Daubert explicitly lists peer review and known error rates as relevant factors — these go to the reliability of the methodology, which is the standard's core inquiry.

stimulus: We should adopt a carbon tax to reduce greenhouse gas emissions because pricing carbon will cause businesses to reduce emissions to avoid the tax.
question: Which of the following is a necessary assumption of this argument?

choices.A: The revenue from the carbon tax should be returned to consumers.
choices.B: Businesses are capable of reducing their emissions if financially motivated to do so.
choices.C: A carbon tax is the most effective policy for reducing emissions.
choices.D: All businesses currently produce greenhouse gas emissions.

explanation: The argument assumes businesses can actually reduce emissions in response to the tax — if they have no feasible way to cut emissions, the tax would not achieve the goal.

stimulus: The best predictor of a country's economic growth is its investment in education. Countries that spend more per pupil consistently outperform those that spend less.
question: Which of the following, if true, most weakens this claim?

choices.A: Countries with higher education spending also tend to have stronger institutions, rule of law, and less corruption — factors independently associated with economic growth.
choices.B: Some countries with low education spending have experienced rapid economic growth.
choices.C: Educational attainment levels vary widely even among countries with similar spending levels.
choices.D: Education spending figures may not capture differences in educational quality.

explanation: If stronger institutions explain both higher education spending and economic growth, then spending on education may not be the causal driver — a confound undermines the 'best predictor' claim.

stimulus: Countries that enacted lockdowns during the pandemic had, on average, higher COVID-19 mortality rates than countries that did not enact lockdowns.
question: Which of the following, if true, best resolves this apparent paradox?

choices.A: Countries that enacted lockdowns tended to do so only after COVID-19 was already spreading rapidly, meaning they already had higher infection and mortality trajectories before the lockdown took effect.
choices.B: Lockdowns damage economic activity, which in turn reduces healthcare funding.
choices.C: Some lockdown countries had older populations, which are more vulnerable to COVID-19.
choices.D: Compliance with lockdown measures varied significantly across countries.

explanation: Selection bias — countries locked down because they were already in worse shape — explains why worse outcomes correlate with lockdowns without indicating lockdowns caused the outcomes.

stimulus: My neighbor switched to a plant-based diet and lost 20 pounds. Therefore, plant-based diets cause weight loss.
question: The argument is flawed because it

choices.A: relies on a single anecdote to establish a causal relationship that may not be generalizable.
choices.B: assumes that weight loss is always a desirable outcome.
choices.C: fails to define what counts as a 'plant-based' diet.
choices.D: does not account for the difficulty of maintaining a plant-based diet.

explanation: A single anecdote cannot establish causation — the neighbor may have also exercised more, reduced calories generally, or had other factors at play.

stimulus: Economists traditionally define rational behavior as maximizing utility. Behavioral economists have demonstrated, however, that people systematically deviate from this model in predictable ways — overweighting losses relative to gains, anchoring decisions to arbitrary reference points, and discounting future rewards at inconsistent rates. These findings suggest that the rational actor model is descriptively inadequate as a basis for economic prediction.
question: The main conclusion of this passage is that

choices.A: people are fundamentally irrational and cannot make good decisions.
choices.B: the traditional economic model of rational behavior fails to accurately describe how people actually make decisions.
choices.C: behavioral economics has replaced traditional economics.
choices.D: loss aversion is the most important cognitive bias in economic decision-making.

explanation: The passage argues that systematic deviations from the rational actor model render it descriptively inadequate — not that people are entirely irrational or that traditional economics is dead.

stimulus: A judge imposes a lighter sentence on a defendant who committed a non-violent crime during a period of severe economic hardship, citing the circumstances as a mitigating factor.
question: Which principle best supports the judge's approach to sentencing?

choices.A: Sentences for non-violent crimes should always be lighter than those for violent crimes.
choices.B: The circumstances surrounding a crime, including economic hardship, are relevant to assessing culpability and should be considered in sentencing.
choices.C: Judges should not consider external circumstances when applying the law.
choices.D: Economic hardship is a complete defense to non-violent crimes.

explanation: The judge used circumstances as a mitigating factor — choice B supports the relevance of context to culpability without excusing the crime entirely.

stimulus: All contracts that lack consideration are voidable at the option of one party. This agreement lacks consideration. Furthermore, all voidable contracts can be ratified by subsequent conduct.
question: Which of the following can be properly inferred?

choices.A: This agreement is void and cannot be enforced.
choices.B: This agreement is voidable and could potentially be ratified.
choices.C: This agreement will automatically be voided by a court.
choices.D: Ratification of this agreement requires a new agreement with consideration.

explanation: The agreement lacks consideration → it is voidable. All voidable contracts can be ratified by subsequent conduct → this one can be ratified. Choice B follows from both premises.

stimulus: Self-driving vehicles will reduce traffic fatalities because the majority of accidents are caused by human error, and autonomous vehicles eliminate human error.
question: Which of the following, if true, most weakens this argument?

choices.A: Autonomous vehicle software can malfunction or be fooled by unusual road conditions, edge cases, or sensor interference — creating a new category of machine error.
choices.B: Self-driving vehicles are currently more expensive than conventional vehicles.
choices.C: Human drivers are more adaptable to unexpected situations than current AI systems.
choices.D: Regulatory approval for self-driving vehicles is still pending in many jurisdictions.

explanation: The argument assumes autonomous vehicles have no errors — if machine error replaces human error (perhaps at comparable rates), the predicted reduction in fatalities would not materialize.

stimulus: Economists argue that rent control policies, despite being intended to help low-income renters, actually reduce the supply of affordable housing.
question: Which of the following most directly supports this claim?

choices.A: Landlords in cities with rent control are more likely to convert rental units to condominiums or allow properties to deteriorate rather than maintain them at controlled rents, reducing the supply of rental housing over time.
choices.B: Rent control policies are politically popular in many cities.
choices.C: Low-income renters benefit from stable, predictable rent payments.
choices.D: Cities without rent control have higher rents on average.

explanation: Landlord behavior in response to rent control — converting units or allowing deterioration — provides a direct mechanism by which rent control reduces housing supply.

stimulus: The fact that crime rates fell after police officers began wearing body cameras proves that body cameras deter crime.
question: The argument is most vulnerable to the criticism that it

choices.A: fails to rule out other factors that may have caused crime rates to fall during the same period.
choices.B: assumes that police officers support the use of body cameras.
choices.C: overstates the magnitude of the crime rate reduction.
choices.D: relies on crime statistics that may be inaccurate.

explanation: Post hoc ergo propter hoc — assuming that because B followed A, A caused B. Without controlling for other variables (economic conditions, other policing changes), the causal attribution is unwarranted.

stimulus: The mayor proposes closing a downtown homeless shelter, arguing that it attracts crime to the surrounding blocks and that property values near shelters are lower than in comparable areas.
question: Which of the following most seriously challenges the mayor's reasoning?

choices.A: Studies controlling for prior neighborhood conditions have found that homeless shelters do not cause increases in crime and that property value differences pre-date shelter openings.
choices.B: The shelter serves over 200 people nightly.
choices.C: Other cities have experimented with closing shelters with mixed results.
choices.D: Property values near shelters have risen in some cities.

explanation: If crime rates don't increase and property value differences pre-existed the shelter, both causal claims in the mayor's argument are undermined simultaneously.

stimulus: The commerce clause of the U.S. Constitution grants Congress the power to regulate commerce among the states. Courts have interpreted this broadly to include not just the movement of goods across state lines, but also activities that substantially affect interstate commerce. This expansive interpretation reached its apex in Wickard v. Filburn (1942), where the Supreme Court held that a farmer growing wheat for his own consumption could be regulated because, in the aggregate, such activity affected national wheat prices.
question: The Wickard decision is most significant for which proposition?

choices.A: Congress may regulate any economic activity, regardless of its scale or effects.
choices.B: Even purely local, non-commercial activity may be regulated under the commerce clause if, in the aggregate across many actors, it substantially affects interstate commerce.
choices.C: The commerce clause grants Congress unlimited legislative power over all economic matters.
choices.D: Farmers who grow food for personal consumption are engaged in interstate commerce.

explanation: Wickard extended commerce clause power to purely local, personal-use activity based on the aggregate effects theory — the individual farmer's wheat affected national prices when aggregated with all similar farmers.

stimulus: Judge Alvarez consistently rules in favor of plaintiffs in discrimination cases. She must be biased against defendant employers.
question: The argument depends on which of the following assumptions?

choices.A: Discrimination cases brought before Judge Alvarez are not meritorious.
choices.B: A judge who rules consistently in favor of one party must be doing so for reasons other than the merits of the cases.
choices.C: Judge Alvarez has a personal history that predisposes her to side with plaintiffs.
choices.D: Other judges rule less consistently in discrimination cases.

explanation: The argument jumps from 'consistent rulings' to 'bias.' This requires assuming consistent outcomes cannot result from the cases consistently having merit — which is the hidden, questionable assumption.
